title: Senate Hopefuls Spar on Data Center Rules
teaser: Stevens and El-Sayed clash on data centers in the Dem Senate debate.

lines.text: You're listening to the Michigan Data Wire — your daily podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Today the Democratic U.S. Senate candidates went head-to-head on how to handle the wave of data center proposals.
That debate sounds like it got right to the heart of what a lot of communities are wrestling with.
Exactly. U.S. Rep. Haley Stevens and Abdul El-Sayed offered pretty different takes during their first one-on-one debate.
What did each of them actually say about the projects themselves?
Both agreed that any development needs community benefit agreements and that residents should have a real voice in decisions.
That lines up with the pushback we've been hearing in townships across the state.
Stevens emphasized protecting Michiganders from higher costs while still competing on technology.
And El-Sayed went further, calling for union labor requirements, no utility rate hikes for residents, water protections, and enforceable agreements.
Right. He framed it as needing strict guardrails before anything moves forward.
So the bigger question is how these positions play out for local electric bills and water supplies.
Data centers can pull massive amounts of power and water, and recent heat and storms already showed how tight the grid is.
Who ends up paying for the upgrades if these projects scale up?
That's exactly the debate — whether ratepayers foot the bill or the companies do through those agreements.
And farmland and small towns are in the middle of it too.
We've seen resistance in places like Washtenaw and Charlevoix counties over exactly those issues.
What happens next after this debate?
Voters will be watching how the candidates turn these positions into policy if they win the primary.
Meanwhile, a new report from the Citizens Research Council is looking at the tax and economic side of these projects.
That one dropped today and examines impacts on local economies.
Also today, GPUS moved forward on a 20-megawatt AI data center deal in Dowagiac that could be worth over a billion dollars.
And in Saline Township, the board is holding a public hearing tonight on a tax exemption for Oracle's big project.
Yesterday a University expert pointed to growing community resistance statewide over water, power, and local control.
A resident also noted how enormous the Saline site looks — basically the size of Michigan International Speedway.
And in Cass County, that same company is advancing plans with major service agreements.
Yesterday John James faced questions about donations from data center builder executives while criticizing the projects.
Hayes Township's advisory group meets tonight to review their draft report on local data center issues.
That's one listeners can actually attend.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker. We're back tomorrow.
